Evolutionary Developmental Biology (Evo-Devo): Past, Present, and Future
========================================================================

*   [Brian K. Hall](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#auth-Brian_K_-Hall-Aff1)[1](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#Aff1)

[_Evolution: Education and Outreach_](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/)**volume 5**,pages 184–193 (2012)[Cite this article](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#citeas)

*   49k Accesses

*   90 Citations

*   16 Altmetric

*   [Metrics details](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x/metrics)

Abstract
--------

Evolutionary developmental biology (evo–devo) is that part of biology concerned with how changes in embryonic development during single generations relate to the evolutionary changes that occur between generations. Charles Darwin argued for the importance of development (embryology) in understanding evolution. After the discovery in 1900 of Mendel’s research on genetics, however, any relationship between development and evolution was either regarded as unimportant for understanding the process(es) of evolution or as a black box into which it was hard to see. Research over the past two decades has opened that black box, revealing how studies in evo–devo highlight the mechanisms that link genes (the genotype) with structures (the phenotype). This is vitally important because genes do not make structures. Developmental processes make structures using road maps provided by genes, but using many other signals as well—physical forces such as mechanical stimulation, temperature of the environment, and interaction with chemical products produced by other species—often species in entirely different kingdoms as in interactions between bacteria and squid or between leaves and larvae (Greene Science 243:643–666, [1989](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR41 "Greene E. A diet-induced developmental polymorphism in a caterpillar. Science. 1989;243:643–66.")). Not only do genes not make structures (the phenotype), but new properties and mechanisms emerge during embryonic development: genes are regulated differentially in different cells and places; aggregations of similar cells provide the cellular resources (modules) from which tissues and organs arise; modules and populations of differently differentiated cells interact to set development along particular tracks; and organisms interact with their environment and create their niche in that environment. Such interactions are often termed “epigenetic,” meaning that they direct gene activity using mechanisms that are not encoded in the DNA of the genes. This paper reviews the origins of evo–devo, how the field has changed over the past 30 years, evaluates the recognition of the importance for development and evolution of mechanisms that are not encoded in DNA, and evaluates what the future might bring for evo–devo. Although impossible to know, history tells us that we might expect more of the same; expansion of evo–devo into other areas of biology (ecology, physiology, behavior); absorption of evo–devo by evolution or a unification of biology in which evo–devo plays a major role.

Introduction
------------

Evolutionary developmental biology (evo–devo) is the name for that part of biology involved in understanding how alterations in the mechanisms of embryonic development influence or direct evolutionary changes in any and all stages of the life cycle. The field has become enormous in recent years; McCain ([2009](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR76 "McCain KW. Using tricitation to dissect the citation image: Conrad Hal Waddington and the rise of evolutionary developmental biology. J Am Soc Inf Sci Technol. 2009;60:1301–19.")) analyzed thousands of papers published between 1975 and 2004 in her study of the impact of one early practitioner of evo–devo, Conrad Hal Waddington. Consequently, what follows is my perspective on the past, present, and future of evo–devo. I apologize in advance to those researchers whose contributions are underrepresented.

Although not usually regarded as a “founding father” of the field, the term “evolutionary developmental biology” appears first to have been used in print in 1983 by the zoologist and later environmentalist, Peter Calow, of the University of Sheffield in England. In an earlier book, Calow ([1978](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR14 "Calow P. Life cycles: an evolutionary approach to the physiology of reproduction, development and ageing. London: Wiley; 1978.")) had provided an influential treatment of life cycles from developmental, evolutionary, and physiological points of view. Calow included a chapter on evolution and development (in itself unusual at the time[Footnote 1](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#Fn1)) in his [1983](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR15 "Calow P. Evolutionary principles. Blackie: Glasgow and London; 1983.") book _Evolutionary Principles_, published by Blackie in their Tertiary Level Biology Series. Calow described the field of evolution and development as “evolutionary developmental biology,” noting that “The area is a relatively new and complex one so the reader should not expect to find fully comprehensive treatments in the literature” (p. 80).

In his book of only 100 pages and 105 references and contrary to much prior opinion, Calow emphasized that natural selection could and did act at any stage in the life cycle of multicellular organisms, a conclusion reached by one of the founders of evolutionary embryology 109 years earlier. In his first paper on shark development, Francis Balfour argued for the role of selection in embryonic development as perhaps even more relevant for evolution than selection on the adult:

> I see no reason for doubting that the embryo in the earliest periods of development is as subject to the laws of natural selection as is the animal at any other period. Indeed, there appear to me grounds for the thinking that it is more so. (Balfour [1874](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR5 "Balfour FM. A preliminary account of the development of the elasmobranch fishes. Q J Microsc Sci. 1874;14:323–64."), p. 343)

Although the roots of evo–devo are deep (Gould [1977](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR40 "Gould SJ. Ontogeny and phylogeny. Cambridge: The Belknap Press of Harvard University Press; 1977."); Bonner [1982](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR10 "Bonner JT. Evolution and development. Report of the Dahlem Workshop on Evolution and Development, Berlin 1981, May 10–15. Life Sciences Research Report 22. Berlin: Springer; 1982."); Arthur [1988](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR3 "Arthur W. A theory of the evolution of development. Chichester: Wiley; 1988."); Hall and Olson [2003](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR55 "Hall K, Olson WM (eds). Keywords and concepts in evolutionary developmental biology. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press; 2003."); Laubichler and Maienschein [2007](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR65 "Laubichler MD, Maienschein J, editors. From Embryology to evo–devo: a history of developmental evolution. Cambridge: MIT Press; 2007."); Olsson et al. [2009](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR86 "Olsson L, Hoßfeld U, Breidbach O. Preface. Between Ernst Haeckel and the homeobox: the role of developmental biology in evolution. Theory Biosci. 2009;128:1–5.")), evo–devo is just coming into its own. In the introduction to the publication of the Kowalevsky Medal winner symposium held in January 2003 (published in 2004) to recognize the research of the first recipients of the Kowalevsky medal, Laubichler and Wagner concluded that “By all accounts ‘evo–devo’ has arrived. It is now solidly entrenched in the conceptual framework of modern biology and has all the markings of a new discipline, such as representation in professional societies, scientific journals devoted to the field, academic programs and job searches, panels at funding agencies, textbooks, etc.” ([2004](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR66 "Laubichler MD, Wagner GP. Introduction to the papers of the 2001 Kowalevsky Medal winner symposium. J Exp Zool (Mol Dev Evol). 2004;302B:1–4."), p. 1) Eight years later, classes, courses, workshops, postdoctoral fellowships, faculty positions, university chairs, and research grant selection panels in evo–devo are widespread.

I took “evolutionary developmental biology” as the title for my 1992 book (Hall [1992](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR45 "Hall BK. Evolutionary developmental biology. London: Chapman and Hall; 1992.")), which set out to summarize what was then known about the origins of the field, its history, and its role in contributing to the evolutionary process. I will do a little of the same in the paper. Why? Because we can only appreciate the future prospects of evo–devo in the context of its past. What questions were being asked of embryos and of development? To which organisms were these questions addressed? Were we seeking a broad overarching theory of evo–devo that would apply to all animal life or differences that characterized grades of biological organization—kingdoms, phyla, classes, even individual species. What was missing from prevalent approaches to evolution that necessitated this new developmental approach?

Origins of Evo-Devo
-------------------

I go back to the late nineteenth century when we find the origins of evo–devo in the research of individuals in England (largely Trinity College, Cambridge) and in Continental Europe. These evolutionary morphologists/evolutionary embryologists were attracted to this research following the publications of _The Origin of Species_ by Charles Darwin (Darwin [1859](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR22 "Darwin CR. The origin of species by means of natural selection. London: John Murray; 1859.")) and Ernst Haeckel’s theory that ontogeny recapitulates phylogeny (Haeckel [1866](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR44 "Haeckel E. Generelle morphologie der organismen: Allgemeine grundzüge der organischen formen-wissenschaft, mechanisch begründet durch die von Charles Darwin reformite descendenz-theorie (2 volumes). Berlin: Georg Reimer; 1866.")). Paradoxically, the first published study testing Darwin’s theory using embryos and larvae—Fritz Müller’s study of crustacean life histories (Müller [1864](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR80 "Müller F. Für Darwin. Leipzig: Engelman; 1864 (Translated by W. S. Dallas as Facts and Arguments for Darwin, London: John Murray, 1869)."))—showed that ontogeny could be used to understand patterns of evolutionary history (phylogeny) and that mechanisms could be sought in ontogeny. So varied were crustacean life history strategies found to be that Müller found he could use the details and varieties of life history stages to construct a phylogeny of crustacean relationships. Haeckel took exactly the opposite position. Haeckel theorized that phylogeny explains ontogeny and erected his Biogenetic Law on this basis.

Embryos provided the way to study evolution. The fossil record was incomplete. Embryos, on the other hand, recorded in their development the history of their ancestors. This history had to be read with great care; there were gaps in the record, and secondary specializations such as the placenta could confuse the unwary (Bowler [1996](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR11 "Bowler PJ. Life's splendid drama. Evolutionary biology and the reconstruction of life's ancestry 1860–1940. Chicago: The University of Chicago Press; 1996."); Hall [1999](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR47 "Hall BK. Evolutionary developmental biology. 2nd ed. Dordrecht: Kluwer Academic; 1999.")). Nevertheless, from the late 1860s or early 1870s until the mid-1880s, evolutionary embryology was the field that attracted the brightest and best zoologists. It attracted those who wanted to study embryos in the laboratory or field station and those who wanted to seek embryos of such ‘missing links’ as the platypus (thought to link reptiles and mammals), lungfish (thought to link fish and tetrapods), and the velvet worm _Peripatus_ (thought to link insects and arthropods) in such exotic places as Australia, South America and Africa (Hall [1999](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR47 "Hall BK. Evolutionary developmental biology. 2nd ed. Dordrecht: Kluwer Academic; 1999."), [2001](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR49 "Hall BK. John Samuel Budgett (1872–1904): in pursuit of Polypterus. BioScience. 2001;51:399–407."); MacLeod [1994](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR72 "Macleod R. Embryology and empire. In: Macleod R, Rehbock PF, editors. Darwin's laboratory. Evolutionary theory and natural history in the Pacific. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press; 1994. p. 140–65."); Bowler [1996](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR11 "Bowler PJ. Life's splendid drama. Evolutionary biology and the reconstruction of life's ancestry 1860–1940. Chicago: The University of Chicago Press; 1996."); Laubichler and Maienschein [2007](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR65 "Laubichler MD, Maienschein J, editors. From Embryology to evo–devo: a history of developmental evolution. Cambridge: MIT Press; 2007.")). William Bateson, the English zoologist who coined the name “genetics,” began his career as an evolutionary embryologist. Reminiscing on his career, Bateson commented that

> Morphology was studied because it was the material believed to be the most favorable for the elucidation of the problems of evolution, and we all thought that in embryology the quintessence of morphological truth was most palpably presented. Therefore every aspiring zoologist was an embryologist, and the one topic of professional conversation was evolution. (Bateson [1922](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR6 "Bateson W. Evolutionary faith and modern doubts. Science. 1922;55:53–61."), p. 56)

Frustration with reconstructing evolutionary trees from embryonic sequences, the rise of experimental and physiological approaches to embryonic development in the 1880s, and the rediscovery of Mendelian genetics in 1900 all cast evolutionary embryology into a backwater from which it would take a century to resurface. Mendel’s principles of segregation and assortment coupled with studies on the fruit fly _Drosophila_ provided a powerful foundation upon which the new science of genetics was built. Publication of Dobzhansky’s ([1937](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR27 "Dobzhansky Th. Genetics and the origin of species. New York: Columbia University Press; 1937.")) influential book, “_Genetics and the Origin of Species_,” provided a basis for understanding evolution through population genetics, the mathematical models for which have been developed in the 1920.

By the middle of the twentieth century, maintenance of the features of organisms, variation in those features, _and_ the origin of new features all seemed explicable by a fusion of Mendelian and population genetics. Paradoxically, the use of _Drosophila_ as the model organism for genetics eliminated the roles of embryonic development and of the environment from evolutionary discussion and theory; inbred laboratory organisms display none of the variation and adaptability seen in nature.

The Twentieth Century
---------------------

Despite the valiant attempts of a handful of individual researchers through the first six decades of the twentieth century, linking embryos and evolution did not make a comeback until Stephen J. Gould’s book _Ontogeny and Phylogeny_ was published in 1977. Weighing in at 501 pages, this was no easy read, especially as the first half was a detailed history of the way in which development and evolution (ontogeny and phylogeny—the terms proposed by Haeckel) had been related in the past and when the past for Gould began at 450 BC. This historical _tour de force_ was important; Gould reminded us, for indeed we had forgotten that we had been seeking relationships between development and evolution for millennia.

The second half of Gould’s book was equally important because it revisited a concept conceived by Haeckel in the 1860s, elaborated by Gavin de Beer in the 1930s (de Beer [1930](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR25 "de Beer GR. Embryos and evolution. Oxford: Clarendon; 1930.")), but then all but abandoned. Haeckel and de Beer’s concept is summed up in the word “heterochrony”—changes in the timing of developmental processes between a descendant and its ancestors (see Zelditch [2001](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR115 "Zelditch M. Beyond heterochrony: the evolution of development. New York: Wiley; 2001."); Willmore [2010](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR113 "Willmore KE. Development influences evolution. Am Sci. 2010;98:220–7.")). De Beer’s insight was to take studies showing that genes affected the rates of physiological processes in insects (Goldschmidt [1918](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR38 "Goldschmidt R. A preliminary report on some genetic experiments concerning evolution. Am Nat. 1918;52:28–50.")) and to see that the rates of development must change during evolution. Here was a way in which comparative embryology could be investigated in an evolutionary (phylogenetic) framework). Heterochrony as a developmental mechanism operating during individual development could be selected for and so was important in evolutionary change; heterochrony was an “evolutionary developmental mechanism” (Hall and Olson [2003](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR55 "Hall K, Olson WM (eds). Keywords and concepts in evolutionary developmental biology. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press; 2003.")).

Julian Huxley, one of the founders of the modern synthesis of evolution, appreciated the importance for evolution of the study of genes in development—“a study of genes during development is as essential for an understanding of evolution as are the study of mutation and selection” (Huxley [1942](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR60 "Huxley JS. Evolution: the modern synthesis. London: Allen and Unwin; 1942."), p. 8)—but even Huxley neglected to incorporate development into evolutionary theory. Other founders of the modern synthesis such as Ernst Mayr spoke of an internal biology (to which development belonged) and an external biology (evolution, ecological interactions) as if the two were separate and non-overlapping/interacting one to the other (Mayr [1982](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR74 "Mayr E. The growth of biological thought. Diversity, evolution, and inheritance. Cambridge: The Belknap Press of Harvard University Press; 1982."), [1997](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR75 "Mayr E. The establishment of evolutionary biology as a discrete biological discipline. BioEssays. 1997;19:263–6.")).

Because it was difficult to compare ancestors and their descendants, living organisms were compared with one another, initially _across broad divisions_ such as phyla and classes—homology of the tissue interactions that initiate the development of Meckel’s cartilage in the lower jaw of amphibians, birds, and mammals (Goodwin et al. [1983](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR39 "Goodwin BC, Holder NJ, Wylie CC, editors. Development and evolution. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press; 1983.")), for example—but increasingly across smaller evolutionary gaps, to the point that _pairs of species_ in the same genus (congeneric species) could be compared

> —loss of the larval stage from one of a pair of congeneric sea urchins (Raff [1992](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR92 "Raff RA. Direct-developing sea urchins and the evolutionary reorganization of early development. BioEssays. 1992;14:211–8.")), for example—

changes in the developmental processes in _inbred strains of a single species_ of laboratory animal could be compared as experiments in evo–devo.

> —changes in the timing (heterochrony) of the tissue interactions responsible for the induction of the lower jaw in three inbred strains of mice (C57BL, C3H/He, CBA/J) by MacDonald and Hall ([2001](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR71 "MacDonald ME, Hall BK. Altered timing of the extracellular-matrix-mediated epithelial–mesenchymal interaction that initiates mandibular skeletogenesis in three inbred strains of mice: development, heterochrony, and evolutionary change in morphology. J Exp Zool. 2001;291:258–73.")), for example—

to the present day when variation in individuals of a single species are revealing the mechanisms underlying the maintenance of natural variation in developmental processes upon which natural selection can act

> —as in the correlation of genetic distance between individuals in relation to variation in the timing of developmental events in the pond snail, _Radix balthica_ by Tills et al. ([2011](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR102 "Tills O, Rundle SD, Salinger M, Haun T, Pfenninger M, Spicer JI. A genetic basis for intraspecific differences in developmental timing? Evol Dev. 2011;13:542–8.")).

Evo–devo exploded as heterochrony was found everywhere. Along the way, heterochrony became such a pervasive term that it lost some of its explanatory power; any change in timing became heterochrony, whether evolutionarily relevant or not (Zelditch [2001](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR115 "Zelditch M. Beyond heterochrony: the evolution of development. New York: Wiley; 2001."), especially pp. vii–ix).

The next major impetus to evo–devo was not the resurrection of a previously known evolutionary developmental mechanisms but the discovery that all animals (subsequently shown for all plants and fungi too) share genes that contain a 180-bp sequence known as the homeobox and that these genes, known as homeobox, homeotic, or Hox genes, are responsible for determining that animals have an anterior and a posterior, a dorsal and a ventral side, and specific regions (often as repeated segments) along the body axis—head at one end tail at the other, thorax in front of abdomen, wings on a specific pair of segments, and so forth (Lewis [1978](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR69 "Lewis EB. A gene complex controlling segmentation in Drosophila. Nature. 1978;276:565–70."); Gehring [1985](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR31 "Gehring WJ. The homeobox: a key to the understanding of development? Cell. 1985;40:3–5."), [1998](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR32 "Gehring WJ. Master control genes in development and evolution: the homeobox story. New Haven: Yale University Press; 1998."); Averof [1997](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR4 "Averof M. Arthropod evolution: same Hox genes, different body plans. Curr Biol. 1997;7:R634–6."); Grenier et al. [1997](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR43 "Grenier JK, Garber TL, Warren R, Whitington PM, Carroll SB. Evolution of the entire arthropod Hox gene set predated the origin and radiation of the onychophoran/arthropod clade. Curr Biol. 1997;7:547–53."); Carroll [2008](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR16 "Carroll SB. Evo–devo and an expanding evolutionary synthesis: a genetic theory of morphological evolution. Cell. 2008;134:25–36.")).

“Master” genes, also known as developmental or regulatory genes, were discovered. One of the best understood of such genes is the paired-box protein gene known as _Pax-6_ in vertebrates and as _eyeless_ (_ey_) in _Drosophila_. As determined from its DNA sequence, orthologues of _Pax-6_ are present throughout the Animal Kingdom. As determined from functional studies, the role of _Pax-6_ in anteriorizing the embryo and in the formation of anterior sensory structures also is conserved across the Animal Kingdom. Although best known as the major gene controlling eye development, _Pax-6_ functions in organisms that lack eyes, reflecting its ancient developmental role. Indeed, _ey_ from fruit flies can initiate eyes in frogs and _Pax-6_ from frogs can initiate eyes in _Drosophila_ (Dahl et al. [1997](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR21 "Dahl E, Koseki H, Balling R. Pax genes and organogenesis. BioEssays. 1997;19:755–65."); Suga et al. [2010](https://evolution-outreach.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1007/s12052-012-0418-x#ref-CR100 "Suga H, Tschopp P, Graziussi DF, Stierwald M, Schmid V, Gehring WJ. Flexibly deployed Pax genes in eye development at the early evolution of animals demonstrated by studies on a hydrozoan jellyfish. Proc Natl Acad Sci USA. 2010;107:14263–8.")). Here is an astonishing and previously unthought-of genetic conservation across animals whose morphology varied enormously.

Heterochrony, homeotic genes, increasingly resolved relationships between organisms (phylogenetic trees), and an appreciation during the 1980s and 1990s of the importance of ecological and species interactions led evo–devo to the position where the aims of evo–devo could be stated as understanding:

*   The origination and evolution of embryonic development;

*   The role of modifications of developmental processes in the production of novel features;

Comparison of organism developmental processes

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Genes_hox.jpeg/500px-Genes_hox.jpeg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Genes_hox.jpeg)

[Homologous](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homology_(biology) "Homology (biology)")[_Hox_ genes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hox_gene "Hox gene") in such different animals as [insects](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Insect "Insect") and [vertebrates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate") control [embryonic development](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Embryogenesis "Embryogenesis") and hence the form of adult bodies. These genes have been [highly conserved](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conserved_sequence "Conserved sequence") through hundreds of millions of years of [evolution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolution "Evolution").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology#cite_note-Carroll_2008-1)

**Evolutionary developmental biology**, informally known as **evo-devo**, is a field of [biological research](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biological_research "Biological research") that compares the [developmental processes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_biology "Developmental biology") of different [organisms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Organism "Organism") to infer how developmental processes [evolved](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolution "Evolution").

The field grew from 19th-century beginnings, where [embryology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Embryology "Embryology") faced a mystery: [zoologists](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zoology "Zoology") did not know how [embryonic development](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Embryogenesis "Embryogenesis") was controlled at the molecular level. [Charles Darwin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin") noted that having similar embryos implied common ancestry, but little progress was made until the 1970s. Then, [recombinant DNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recombinant_DNA "Recombinant DNA") technology at last brought embryology together with [molecular genetics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Molecular_genetics "Molecular genetics"). A key early discovery was that of [homeotic genes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homeotic_gene "Homeotic gene") that regulate development in a wide range of [eukaryotes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote").

The field is composed of multiple core evolutionary concepts. One is [deep homology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deep_homology "Deep homology"), the finding that dissimilar organs such as the eyes of [insects](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Insect "Insect"), [vertebrates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate") and [cephalopod](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cephalopod "Cephalopod") molluscs, long thought to have evolved separately, are controlled by similar genes such as _[pax-6](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pax-6 "Pax-6")_, from the [evo-devo gene toolkit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evo-devo_gene_toolkit "Evo-devo gene toolkit"). These genes are ancient, being [highly conserved](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conserved_sequence "Conserved sequence") among [phyla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phylum "Phylum"); they generate the patterns in time and space which shape the embryo, and ultimately form the [body plan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Body_plan "Body plan") of the organism. Another is that species do not differ much in their structural genes, such as those coding for [enzymes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme"); what does differ is the way that [gene expression is regulated](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regulation_of_gene_expression "Regulation of gene expression") by the [toolkit genes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evo-devo_gene_toolkit "Evo-devo gene toolkit"). These genes are reused, unchanged, many times in different parts of the embryo and at different stages of development, forming a complex cascade of control, switching other regulatory genes as well as structural genes on and off in a precise pattern. This multiple [pleiotropic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pleiotropy "Pleiotropy") reuse explains why these genes are highly conserved, as any change would have many adverse consequences which [natural selection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_selection "Natural selection") would oppose.

New [morphological](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morphology_(biology) "Morphology (biology)") features and ultimately new species are produced by variations in the toolkit, either when genes are expressed in a new pattern, or when toolkit genes acquire additional functions. Another possibility is the [neo-Lamarckian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neo-Lamarckism "Neo-Lamarckism") theory that [epigenetic changes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics "Epigenetics") are later [consolidated at gene level](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Genetic_assimilation "Genetic assimilation"), something that may have been important early in the history of multicellular life.

History

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Evolutionary_developmental_biology&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [History of evolutionary thought](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_evolutionary_thought "History of evolutionary thought")

### Early theories

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Evolutionary_developmental_biology&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early theories")]

Further information: [Aristotle's biology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aristotle%27s_biology "Aristotle's biology")

Philosophers began to think about how animals acquired form in the [womb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Womb "Womb") in [classical antiquity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"). [Aristotle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") asserts in his _[Physics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Physics\_(Aristotle) "Physics (Aristotle)")_ treatise that according to [Empedocles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Empedocles "Empedocles"), order "spontaneously" appears in the developing embryo. In his _[The Parts of Animals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The\_Parts\_of\_Animals "The Parts of Animals")_ treatise, he argues that Empedocles' theory was wrong. In Aristotle's account, Empedocles stated that the [vertebral column](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vertebral_column "Vertebral column") is divided into vertebrae because, as it happens, the embryo twists about and snaps the column into pieces. Aristotle argues instead that the process has a predefined goal: that the "seed" that develops into the embryo began with an inbuilt "potential" to become specific body parts, such as vertebrae. Further, each sort of animal gives rise to animals of its own kind: humans only have human babies.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology#cite_note-Leroi_2014-2)

### Recapitulation

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Evolutionary_developmental_biology&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Recapitulation")]

Main article: [Recapitulation theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recapitulation_theory "Recapitulation theory")

[![Image 6](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Haeckel_vs_von_Baer.svg/500px-Haeckel_vs_von_Baer.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Haeckel_vs_von_Baer.svg)

Embryology theories of [Ernst Haeckel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ernst_Haeckel "Ernst Haeckel"), who argued for [recapitulation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recapitulation_theory "Recapitulation theory")[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology#cite_note-3) of evolutionary development in the embryo, and [Karl Ernst von Baer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karl_Ernst_von_Baer "Karl Ernst von Baer")'s [epigenesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenesis_(biology) "Epigenesis (biology)")

A [recapitulation theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recapitulation_theory "Recapitulation theory") of evolutionary development was proposed by [Étienne Serres](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Serres "Étienne Serres") in 1824–26, echoing the 1808 ideas of [Johann Friedrich Meckel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Johann_Friedrich_Meckel "Johann Friedrich Meckel"). They argued that the embryos of 'higher' animals went through or recapitulated a series of stages, each of which resembled an animal lower down the [great chain of being](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_chain_of_being "Great chain of being"). For example, the brain of a human embryo looked first like that of a [fish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fish "Fish"), then in turn like that of a [reptile](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reptile "Reptile"), [bird](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bird "Bird"), and [mammal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mammal "Mammal") before becoming clearly [human](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human "Human"). The embryologist [Karl Ernst von Baer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karl_Ernst_von_Baer "Karl Ernst von Baer") opposed this, arguing in 1828 that there was no linear sequence as in the great chain of being, based on a single [body plan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Body_plan "Body plan"), but a process of [epigenesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenesis_(biology) "Epigenesis (biology)") in which structures differentiate. Von Baer instead recognized four distinct animal [body plans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Body_plan "Body plan"): radiate, like [starfish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Starfish "Starfish"); molluscan, like [clams](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clam "Clam"); articulate, like [lobsters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lobster "Lobster"); and vertebrate, like fish. Zoologists then largely abandoned recapitulation, though [Ernst Haeckel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ernst_Haeckel "Ernst Haeckel") revived it in 1866.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology#cite_note-4)[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology#cite_note-5)[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology#cite_note-6)[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology#cite_note-7)[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology#cite_note-8)

### Evolutionary morphology

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Evolutionary_developmental_biology&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Evolutionary morphology")]

Further information: [Morphology (biology)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morphology_(biology) "Morphology (biology)") and [Body plan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Body_plan "Body plan")

[![Image 7](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Comparison_of_Three_Invertebrate_Chordates.svg/250px-Comparison_of_Three_Invertebrate_Chordates.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Comparison_of_Three_Invertebrate_Chordates.svg)

A. [Lancelet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lancelet "Lancelet") (a chordate), B. Larval [tunicate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tunicate "Tunicate"), C. Adult tunicate. [Kowalevsky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alexander_Kovalevsky "Alexander Kovalevsky") saw that the [notochord](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Notochord "Notochord") (1) and gill slits (5) are shared by tunicates and vertebrates.

From the early 19th century through most of the 20th century, [embryology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Embryology "Embryology") faced a mystery. Animals were seen to develop into adults of widely differing [body plan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Body_plan "Body plan"), often through similar stages, from the egg, but zoologists knew almost nothing about how [embryonic development](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Embryogenesis "Embryogenesis") was controlled at the [molecular level](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Molecular_biology "Molecular biology"), and therefore equally little about how [developmental processes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_biology "Developmental biology") had evolved.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology#cite_note-CarrollNatHist-9)[Charles Darwin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin") argued that a shared embryonic structure implied a common ancestor. For example, Darwin cited in his 1859 book _[On the Origin of Species](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/On\_the\_Origin\_of\_Species "On the Origin of Species")_ the [shrimp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shrimp "Shrimp")-like [larva](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Larva "Larva") of the [barnacle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barnacle "Barnacle"), whose [sessile](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sessility_(motility) "Sessility (motility)") adults looked nothing like other [arthropods](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arthropods "Arthropods"); [Linnaeus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carl_Linnaeus "Carl Linnaeus") and [Cuvier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cuvier "Cuvier") had classified them as [molluscs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mollusc "Mollusc").[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology#cite_note-Gilbert2003-10)[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology#cite_note-11) Darwin also noted [Alexander Kowalevsky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alexander_Kovalevsky "Alexander Kovalevsky")'s finding that the [tunicate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tunicate "Tunicate"), too, was not a mollusc, but in its larval stage had a [notochord](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Notochord "Notochord") and pharyngeal slits which developed from the same germ layers as the equivalent structures in [vertebrates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate"), and should therefore be grouped with them as [chordates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chordates "Chordates").[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology#cite_note-Gilbert2003-10)[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology#cite_note-12)

19th century zoology thus converted [embryology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Embryology "Embryology") into an evolutionary science, connecting [phylogeny](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phylogeny "Phylogeny") with [homologies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homology_(biology) "Homology (biology)") between the germ layers of embryos. Zoologists including [Fritz Müller](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fritz_M%C3%BCller "Fritz Müller") proposed the use of embryology to discover [phylogenetic relationships](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phylogeny "Phylogeny") between taxa. Müller demonstrated that [crustaceans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crustaceans "Crustaceans") shared the [Nauplius](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crustacean_larvae#Nauplius "Crustacean larvae") larva, identifying several parasitic species that had not been recognized as crustaceans. Müller also recognized that [natural selection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_selection "Natural selection") must act on larvae, just as it does on adults, giving the lie to recapitulation, which would require larval forms to be shielded from natural selection.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_developmental_biology#cite_note-Gilbert2003-10) Two of Haeckel's other ideas about the evolution of development have fared better than recapitulation: he argued in the 1870s that changes in the timing ([heterochrony](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heterochrony "Heterochrony")) and changes in the positioning within the body ([heterotopy](https://en.wikipedia.org/w

## Abstract

The early studies of evolutionary developmental biology (Evo-Devo) come from several sources. Tributaries flowing into Evo-Devo came from such disciplines as embryology, developmental genetics, evolutionary biology, ecology, paleontology, systematics, medical embryology and mathematical modeling. This essay will trace one of the major pathways, that from evolutionary embryology to Evo-Devo and it will show the interactions of this pathway with two other sources of Evo-Devo: ecological developmental biology and medical developmental biology. Together, these three fields are forming a more inclusive evolutionary developmental biology that is revitalizing and providing answers to old and important questions involving the formation of biodiversity on Earth. The phenotype of Evo-Devo is limited by internal constraints on what could be known given the methods and equipment of the time and it has been framed by external factors that include both academic and global politics.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Evo-Devo emerged in the late 19th century, integrating embryology and evolutionary theory, notably influenced by Darwin's ideas on common ancestry and the importance of developmental processes.
Key concepts include deep homology, regulatory networks, and heterochrony, illustrating how genetic mechanisms shape development and evolutionary change across diverse species.

Case studies, such as Pax-6 in eye development, reveal genetic conservation across distantly related species, highlighting the role of evolutionary developmental biology in understanding morphological diversity.
Evo-Devo reshapes evolutionary biology by emphasizing the interplay of genetic and environmental factors, challenging traditional views on the evolution of traits and species.
Future research may explore epigenetic influences, environmental interactions, and the evolution of complex life cycles, further integrating ecological and developmental perspectives.
